coredata.dc:title: Color-tunable multilayered laminated luminescent solar concentrators based on colloidal quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xin
Benetti, Daniele
Liu, Jiabin
Jin, Lei
Rosei, Federico
coredata.dc:description:
Luminescent solar concentrators (LSC) are a promising technology for building integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). Besides obtaining satisfactory efficiencies and aesthetic appeal, a simple and low-cost fabrication process is required to deploy large-scale LSCs. Herein, we introduced a facile and cost-effective fabrication method to obtain laminated LSC (L-LSC) devices. By optimizing the fabrication parameters, L-LSC based on polymer substrates exhibited higher efficiency (power conversion efficiency, PCE=0.24%, external photon efficiency, η
ext =4.14% and optical efficiency, η
opt =3.46%) compared to glass-based L-LSC (PCE=0.09%, η
ext =1.59%,
η
opt
=1.76%) due to their excellent optical properties and good compatibility with the laminated emissive layer. Using the optimized L-LSC device, a color-tunable multilayered L-LSC structure is then proposed. By embedding different QDs into two interlayers, the color appearance of the device can be tuned, simultaneously improving the overall efficiency. This study presents a notable advancement towards a practical application of L-LSCs with a satisfactory efficiency and color aesthetics for sustainable BIPVs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Single-interlayered L-LSC fabrication process: (a) Commercial PMMA sheets as the substrates and small (1 × 1 mm2) rubber pieces (or QDs based PLMA pieces) as spacers in four corners, and four edges were sealed by a black tape. (b) As-prepared L-LSC mold for casting. (c) L-LSCs (perovskite QDs as luminophores) under room light. (d) L-LSCs under simulated sunlight (AM 1.5 G). The one on the bottom is a large-sized L-LSC with the area of 10 × 10 cm2.

(a) Photographs of representative L-LSCs under room light (top) and simulated solar radiation (bottom). The L-LSC on the left is based on PMMA, while the one on the right is based on commercial green glasses. (b) Transmittance of L-LSCs. (c) J-V curves of Si solar cell mounted on one edge of each L-LSC and polymer LSC, (d) Average EQE spectra of representative L-LSCs, and corresponding integrated short-circuit current density.

Comparison of interlayer thickness in L-LSCs (PMMA as substrates): (a) Fabricated L-LSCs with different interlayer thicknesses (0.5 mm, 1 mm, 2 mm) under room light (up) and UV radiation (down). (b) Transmittance of L-LSCs with different interlayer thicknesses. (c) J-V curves of Si solar cell mounted on the edges of thickness-tuned L-LSCs. (d) Average EQE spectra of L-LSCs, and corresponding integrated short-circuit current density. (e) Optical efficiencies of L-LSCs with different thicknesses (PMMA as substrates). Both experimental and simulated data are shown to investigate the effect of thickness variations on optical efficiencies. The simulation data are obtained by Monte-Carlo ray tracing method. (f) Experimental and simulated optical efficiencies of conventional polymer LSCs.

Fabricated color-tunable multilayered L-LSCs: (a) Scheme of single-interlayered L-LSC (3 layers in total) and double-interlayered L-LSC (5 layers in total). (b) Absorption and emission spectra of blue (B), green (G) and red (R) perovskites QDs. (c) Pure primary color based single-interlayered L-LSCs under room light (top), UV radiation (bottom) and simulated AM 1.5 G (right). (d) Double-interlayered L-LSCs with blue and red QDs (separated-BR L-LSC). Blue and red QDs were embedded into PLMA as first and second interlayer, respectively. (e) Double-interlayered L-LSCs with blue and green QDs (separated-BG L-LSC). Blue and green QDs were embedded into PLMA as first and second interlayer, respectively. (f) Double-interlayered L-LSCs with green and red QDs (separated-GR L-LSC). Green and red QDs were embedded into PLMA as first and second interlayer, respectively. (g) Physically mixed of two types of QDs in PLMA as the only emissive interlayer in L-LSCs: mixed green and red QDs (Mixed-GR), mixed blue and red QDs (Mixed-BR), mixed blue and green QDs (Mixed-BG). In d-f, L-LSCs are illuminated by simulated solar radiation, while in the inset photographs UV radiation is used.

Optical properties and performance of multilayered L-LSCs: (a) J-V curves of the mounted solar cells on the device edges (5 cm × 0.7 cm for double-interlayered L-LSCs with separated QDs in the interlayers, and 5 cm × 0.45 cm for single-interlayered L-LSCs with mixed QDs. (b) Possible photons propagation mechanisms in multilayered L-LSCs. (c) Transmittance of as-fabricated multilayered L-LSCs. (d) (a*, b*) Coordinates of multilayered L-LSCs in CIELAB color space.
Schematic representation of five types of planar LSCs.

head.ce:title: Color-tunable multilayered laminated luminescent solar concentrators based on colloidal quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Energy, Materials and Telecommunications, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique, 1650 Boulevard Lionel-Boulet, Varennes, Québec J3X 1P7, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Energy, Materials and Telecommunications, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Luminescent solar concentrators (LSC) are a promising technology for building integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). Besides obtaining satisfactory efficiencies and aesthetic appeal, a simple and low-cost fabrication process is required to deploy large-scale LSCs. Herein, we introduced a facile and cost-effective fabrication method to obtain laminated LSC (L-LSC) devices. By optimizing the fabrication parameters, L-LSC based on polymer substrates exhibited higher efficiency (power conversion efficiency, PCE=0.24%, external photon efficiency, η
ext =4.14% and optical efficiency, η
opt =3.46%) compared to glass-based L-LSC (PCE=0.09%, η
ext =1.59%,
η
opt
=1.76%) due to their excellent optical properties and good compatibility with the laminated emissive layer. Using the optimized L-LSC device, a color-tunable multilayered L-LSC structure is then proposed. By embedding different QDs into two interlayers, the color appearance of the device can be tuned, simultaneously improving the overall efficiency. This study presents a notable advancement towards a practical application of L-LSCs with a satisfactory efficiency and color aesthetics for sustainable BIPVs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the world's population continues to grow and urbanise, the demand for clean energy for sustainable society development is increasing. Simultaneously, the contradiction between supplies and demands of urban land has become increasingly acute, leading to vertical urbanization and higher urban skylines [1,2].

In this context, building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPVs), which consists of solar harvesting systems for electrical power generation embedded seamlessly in high rise buildings, has shown significant potential towards the realization of “zero-energy/self-powered” sustainable urban architectures [3–5]. Their aesthetics play an important role in their integration into buildings [3], however, traditional BIPVs based on silicon solar panels present several limitations, such as heavy and rigid modules, lack of modulable aesthetics and limited absorption of diffused light. In particular, using silicon cells it is difficult to maintain high transparency with acceptable color appearance, while achieving satisfactory efficiencies. These issues represent a major barrier for the commercialization of silicon-based BIPVs [6].

Conversely, luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) are promising for BIPVs, as they satisfy both power generation and aesthetic needs [7]. In general, an LSC is a polymer or/and glass based optical waveguide with luminophores (e.g. organic dyes, quantum dots) embedded in it [8–10]. Upon illumination, the luminophores absorb both direct and diffused sunlight and reemit it at longer wavelengths via downshifting. Through total internal reflection (TIR), the reemitted photons then propagate to the edges of LSCs. Coupled with PV cells at these edges, LSCs convert the concentrated photon flux into electricity [7,8]. Compared to conventional PV cells, LSC present higher modularity, as it is possible to make them semi- or fully transparent. Their color and shape can be controlled, making them even flexible [11–13]. Such advantages make LSCs a promising candidate for several applications, including BIPV [14–17].

So far, the modest performance of LSCs has limited their widespread use. To obtain high-performance LSCs, it is necessary to reduce the energy losses by controlling several parameters. In particular, the luminophores, the waveguide materials, and the structures of the LSC play all a fundamental role in determining the overall efficiency of the device. Luminophores occupy a prominent position in the downshifting of the solar photons. Among numerous options, colloidal quantum dots (QDs) are particularly interesting as they possess several advantages such as high tunability of their optical properties due to quantum confinement, relatively high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and large Stokes shift [18–25].

The waveguide is another key component in LSCs, as it is used as host material to embed the luminophores. Cost-effective polymers, such as poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), poly(lauryl methacrylate) (PLMA), have been widely exploited for LSC applications due to their excellent transparency, high refractive indexes and good compatibility with luminophores [26,27]. Based on the various waveguide materials, in general, five types of architectures for planar LSCs have been developed so far ( Scheme 1): full polymer LSCs [21,28,29], top-coated thin film LSCs [19,30–34], liquid LSCs [35–37], tandem LSCs [38–41], and laminated LSCs [42–46]. Full polymer LSCs are manufactured in pre-fabricated molds by casting the polymer solution containing the luminophores. The solution can be then polymerized into the solid phase using UV-light. This polymerization process requires a UV/heating initiator that needs to be kept under UV/heating radiation for several hours to complete the phase transfer, which may have a detrimental effect on the QDs (e.g. QDs emission quenching) [47,48]. Top-coated thin film LSCs are formed by coating polymer thin films on substrates (e.g. commercial glass/acrylic plate) [21,28,32,49–51]. This structure can be obtained by doctor blade techniques, allowing the easy fabrication of large area LSCs. To avoid the QDs emission quenching from the polymerization process, liquid LSCs have been developed by replacing the polymers with a liquid medium [35–37]. Tandem LSCs are usually formed by two or more full polymers and/or top-coated thin film LSCs to extend the devices absorption band for enhanced device efficiencies. Finally, laminated LSCs (L-LSCs) are manufactured into sandwich-like laminated structures, where the polymer emissive interlayer is inserted between two optically clear waveguides [45,52–55].

Recently, it was reported that the laminated structures for LSCs could help to increase efficiencies due to the use of optically clear waveguides, and improve their aesthetics by stacking different interlayers [42,53,56]. Generally, the LSC structures should have a favorable overall thickness (5 mm is considered the optimum) [57] to reduce re-absorption effects and effectively contact the mounted PV cells. For full polymer LSCs, a complex polymerization process is required to complete the phase transformation, which inevitably induces polymer impurities, QDs aggregation, and possible surface defects/polymer self-absorption. These unexpected impurities and defects have a negative effect on final device efficiency [53]. Instead, the use of optically clear waveguide substrates in L-LSCs can limit the aforementioned energy losses, as they act as the major propagation paths of reemitted light. Moreover, the substrates at the top and bottom could act as protection layers, preventing the interaction with outside environments (e.g. humidity) for better stability and longer service life [43], and also provide a rigid support for some polymer interlayers with poor stiffness (e.g. PLMA). L-LSCs with a multilayered structure could represent one of the most promising and practical LSCs architectures for BIPV, as they are based on the similar architecture of double glass windows already utilized in buildings. Further, as a matter of environmental concern, compared to full polymer waveguides that require large amounts of polymers containing QDs, L-LSC allow a reduction in the amount of unrecyclable QDs mixed polymer materials needed, thus reducing the environmental footprint of the device.

So far, several L-LSC fabrication methods have been reported. McDaniel et.al introduced a capillary injection method to cast the liquid resin containing colloidal QDs between two glasses [42,53], and through an iterative process built multi-interlayered L-LSCs. Zhao et.al used a casting mold with a silicon rubber spacer between two pieces of substrates (glass, PMMA and quartz). However, the fabricated LSC presented several airgaps and an irregular shape at the edges, due to the removal of the spacers [43]. The same group also reported a drop-casting & heating-pressing method to first coat a PVP film with carbon dots thin film on a glass, followed by pressing another piece of glass on the top under heating to make a L-LSC [58]. This type of heating process is difficult to control, often causing bubbles and cavities in L-LSCs. Verduzco et al. designed a homemade stainless-steel mold to compress an emissive polymer between two pieces of acrylic sheets, where the maximum size of L-LSC depends on the mold dimensions [46]. Sychugov et.al introduced a polymer offstoichiometricthiol-ene (OSTE) with Si QDs based L-LSCs, yet the procedure used for building laminated structures is not reported in detail [54]. So far, there are no reports that systematically investigate the parameters (substrates, interlayer thickness, concentration of QDs, etc.) to optimize colloidal QDs based L-LSCs and study their effect on device performance.

With respect to aesthetic quality, it is vital to integrate LSC with BIPVs in a “acceptable” way without affecting urban landscapes and building appearances, which is also a key element to improve their public acceptance and ultimately realize their commercialisation [3,59,60]. Thus, to provide multiply aesthetic choices, appearances and color customization of LSCs should be taken into consideration when LSCs performance is evaluated [61]. However, so far, only few studies investigated the aesthetic quality of LSCs [30,42], with no reports on color tunable laminated LSCs.

In this work, we describe step by step a cost-effective method to fabricate colloidal QDs based L-LSCs with large areas. To identify the optimum conditions for the highest power conversion efficiency (PCE), a systematic investigation of several parameters and materials (substrates and thickness of the waveguides) has been carried out. Subsequently, we developed a multilayered L-LSC based on perovskite QDs with tunable “colors” (blue, green, and red). The designed multilayered L-LSCs not only exhibit improved PCE compared to the single LSC components, but it also allows to alter the final appearance of the device. The results show that the designed double-interlayered L-LSCs achieves PCE values of 0.30 ∼ 0.34% and optical efficiencies (
η
opt
) of 4.30–4.97%, with a maximum two-fold improvement compared to single-interlayer L-LSCs (PCE=0.10–0.20%,
η
opt
=2.05–3.15%). Further, the double-interlayered L-LCS maintains a satisfactory average visible transparency (AVT), between 53.0% and 63.50%. Overall, the proposed manufacturing method and optimization process for multilayered structures can be universally and easily applied to other types of LSCs devices and QDs, allowing to optimize both their optical performance and their aesthetics.

The method presented here introduces an easy, cost-effective and time-saving fabrication process to obtain large-area L-LSC with equipment that is easily accessible to any chemistry lab. No specially designed molds are required, and the basic materials for building the L-LSCs are all based on commercial low-cost materials (the costs are reported in Table S1). This method is universally applicable to several colloidal QDs which can be uniformly dispersed in different polymers (i.e. PLMA and PMMA) while maintaining their optical properties [20,21,35]. The procedure to fabricate a typical single-interlayered L-LSC is illustrated in Fig. 1a (the details are shown in supporting information (SI)). Two clear optical substrates are separated by four “spacers” (silicon rubber or QDs based PLMA polymers, 1 × 1 mm2) to form the casting cavity. Subsequently, black tapes are used to seal the edges of the mold for preventing leakage of polymer solution [45], while a small hole is reserved for injecting the QDs based polymer solution. Fig. 1b shows the well-sealed laminated molds. The mold is a hollow structure that is covered with black tape to prevent the solution from leaking when injected. The injection occurs through a small hole located in one corner, which is larger than the syringe needle to ensure sufficient space for air to escape. Prior to the polymerization process, it is necessary to gently shake the mold to remove any air that may be trapped in the solution. Perovskites CsPb(Br0.2I0.8)3 QDs were chosen as typical luminophores in L-LSCs due to their relatively high PLQY (∼60%) and relatively large Stokes shift compared to other types of inorganic lead halide perovskite QDs, exhibiting excellent performance [62]. Their optical properties are shown in Fig. S1, showing a broad absorption spectrum (300–660 nm) and a sharp emission peak at 658 nm. The QDs based PLMA solution was injected the cavity, followed by UV irradiation for 0.5–2 h to complete the phase transformation. After the polymerization, a single-interlayered L-LSC can be obtained. Fig. 1c-d show the as-fabricated CsPb(Br0.2I0.8)3 QDs based single-interlayered L-LSCs. The surfaces and edges are flat and smooth without requiring further post-treatments (cutting and polishing). By applying this method, L-LSCs with large area can be easily obtained by simply using larger substrates, an illustration of which can be found in Fig. 1d (LSC size of 10 × 10 cm2). The substrates and interlayer thickness can be tuned by changing the different materials plate and adjusting the spacer thickness, respectively, both of which influence L-LSC performance.

So far, various waveguide materials, such as PMMA, green glass, low-iron glass etc., have been tested [63–65]. However, further research is necessary to thoroughly evaluate and compare the performance of these materials in laminated structures under the same conditions. For this reason, here we selected commercial green glass ( n =1.51) and PMMA sheets ( n =1.49) with both thickness of 2 mm as the candidates for L-LSC external layers, as they exhibit excellent optical properties and are cost-effective in large quantities. Low-iron glass ( n =1.51) exhibits excellent performance when used for L-LSC [53], however, its price is almost twice as high compared to that of green glass and PMMA (Table S1). In this work, we tested its optical parameters only for fair comparison. PLMA ( n = 1.50) was chosen as the interlayer material due to its high transparency, similar refractive index with the substrates, and excellent compatibility with the QDs. To remove the concentration effect of the QDs and keep the absorption of each device constant, the amount of QDs in each device (5 ×5 cm2) was fixed at 7 mg. Compared to green glass, both PMMA sheet and low-iron glass presented better transparencies ( Fig. 2b), yielding almost the same average visible transmittances (AVT) (91.92% and 92.10%, respectively), while green glass had a slightly lower AVT of 90.09%. In Fig. 2a are reported perovskites-based L-LSCs with an interlayer thickness of 0.5 mm, manufactured by PMMA (left) and green glass (right) under day light and simulated solar radiation (AM 1.5 G), respectively. It is found that green glass-based device (L-LSC-glass) showed a darker tinted surface appearance due to the iron impurity in the waveguide, while a lower average visible transmittance (AVT) of 79.96% is obtained compared to that of PMMA based L-LSC (L-LSC-PMMA, 84.23%) (Fig. 2a and Table S2). We also fabricated an LSC based only on polymer, with a thickness of 2 mm, a common value for QDs-LSC devices [21,35,41,66–69]. The full polymer LSC with a 2 mm thickness (LSC-2 mm) presents a lower VAT of 83.68% than L-LSC-PMMA with overall thickness of 4.5 mm (4 mm from substrates and 0.5 mm from QDs interlayer), which can probably be attributed to polymer impurities and QDs aggregation during polymerization in bare polymer environment [70].

To calculate the efficiency of the devices, the current density-voltage (J-V) curves of as-prepared L-LSCs using different substrates were obtained under simulated solar illumination (AM 1.5 G), as shown in Fig. 2c. The conventional full polymer device, LSC-2 mm, exhibits a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 0.14%, and an optical efficiency (
η
opt
) of 2.68%. Instead, the laminated structure with PMMA, L-LSC-PMMA, presents enhanced performance, yielding a PCE of 0.24% and
η
opt
of 3.46%. Finally, the PCE of the L-LSC-glass using green glass as substrates was only 0.09% (
η
opt
=1.76%).

According to a previous report, compared to PMMA, glass-based L-LSC with a slightly higher refractive index showed a higher optical efficiency [43]. In our work we observed an opposite trend, with the L-LSC-glass exhibiting a lower performance compared to L-LSC-PMMA. This result can be explained by the low transparency of the commercial green glass used in our work that reduces the amount of light absorbed by the QDs, and also by the presence of airgaps/bubbles between the polymer interlayer and the glasses (Fig. S2a), which thereby changes the TIR light path to increase the overall energy loss (Fig. S2b).

For consistency check, we measured the average position-dependent external quantum efficiency (EQE) for LSC-PV system [71]. The obtained EQE results and the corresponding integrated short-circuit current density (
J
sc
Int
) are shown in Fig. 2d. For all the devices, the EQE curves in the 300–700 nm region are in good agreement with the perovskite QD absorption spectra, indicating that these wavelengths originate from the luminophores’ absorption and contribute to the PCE. In addition, for the samples LSC-2 mm and L-LSC-PMMA, some less intense peaks are observed in the 700 ∼1100 nm region. According to the literature, [61] an excitation beam that is not well-focused can introduce a diverging effect on the tested device, resulting in excessive absorption around 700–1100 nm and overestimation of the integrated current density. In our experiments, however, we ensured that the test platform supporting the devices was fixed in a specific position to maintain a well-focused excitation beam with no divergence. Despite this, we only found peaks at 700–1100 nm in the device with PLMA-containing QDs. In Fig. S3, the comparison result shows that these peaks are not caused by a divergent excitation beam, but rather by the scattering effect from possible QD aggregation and/or polymer impurities. These details have been added to the main text. Such peaks are not caused by the so-called divergent excitation beam during the measurement (Fig. S3), but are probably due to the scattering effect resulting from the QD aggregations and/or polymer impurities, which has been proved to contribute beneficially to LSC performance [72,73]. The
J
sc
Int
values of LSC-2 mm, L-LSC-glass and L-LSC-PMMA are 0.92, 0.51 and 1.30 mA cm−2, respectively, which are consistent with the JSC results from J-V curves (in Table S2). Due to their excellent performance in L-LSC, PMMA was then selected as the substrate to further optimize the device interlayer thickness.

To investigate the effect of interlayer thickness on L-LSC performance, several single-interlayered L-LSCs based on PMMA were prepared. The amount of perovskite QDs for each device is again fixed at a value of 7 mg. Fig. 3a are photographs of as-fabricated L-LSCs with different interlayer thicknesses (0.5 mm, 1.0 mm and 2.0 mm). All the L-LSCs present a similar top-surface appearance under daylight or UV radiation, with no obvious color difference, while the interlayer thickness difference is visible to the naked eye. The transmittance spectra of the devices were then collected (Fig. 3b). In this case, the increase of interlayer thickness in L-LSCs could slightly reduce the device transparency, with AVT values of 82.18%, 80.70% and 78.65% for L-LSC-0.5 mm, L-LSC-1 mm and L-LSC-2 mm, respectively. We further evaluated their aesthetic quality under solar light by using the CIELAB color space coordinate (a*,b*), following a previous report [74]. Fig. S4 shows the colors of L-LSCs under solar light illumination and their corresponding coordinates in CIELAB color space. The increase of device interlayer thickness has little effect on a* variation which (- 0.49∼ - 0.13), but leads to a slight change of b* from 11.82 to 14.19. The increase of b* value indicates a color shifting from blue to yellow, which could reduce the aesthetic appeal for BIPV applications.

We then evaluated the optical and PCE performance of L-LSCs with different thickness, and the J-V curves are shown in Fig. 3d. The L-LSCs with the highest interlayer thickness of 2 mm resulted in a slightly improved PCE and
η
opt
, with a value of 0.29% and 4.32%, respectively, while L-LSC-0.5 mm and L-LSC-1 mm achieved the PCE values of 0.24% and 0.28%,
η
opt
of 3.46% and 4.03%, respectively. Overall, the devices presented similar performances, with L-LSC-2 mm exhibiting the highest J
SC (1.37 ± 0.06 mA cm−2), consistent with the result from average EQE spectra and the corresponding
J
sc
Int
(1.45 mA cm−2) shown in Fig. 4d and Table S2. The average EQE curve of L-LSC-2 mm at around 400 nm presented the lowest value, indicating some energy losses. We hypothesized that such losses could be attributed to an increased amount of polymer impurities and defects due to thicker interlayer. However, this part of energy loss was compensated by the scattering effect at 700–1100 nm and finally a best LSC performance was achieved with L-LSC-2 mm.

To quantitatively analyse the light propagation in L-LSCs with different interlayer thicknesses, we conducted Monte-Carlo ray-tracing simulation. We calculated the ratio of incident photons to output photons from four edges of LSC/L-LSC as the simulated optical efficiency, and the experimental optical efficiencies were also collected for comparison (see details in SI). The total mass of QDs per each LSC/L-LSC was fixed at 7 mg to keep all the devices with the same absorption ability (i.e. same absorbance). The experimentally collected absorption spectra of the devices are shown in Fig. S5. The simulated optical efficiencies of L-LSCs increased as the interlayer thicknesses increase (Fig. 3e), confirming the experimentally observed values. As the absorption is maintained constant among the devices, this trend can be ascribed to the reduced re-absorption loss in thicker L-LSCs. To further understand the effect of LSC thickness on device optical efficiencies, we conducted a simulation on the conventional full polymer LSC which has the same absorption as L-LSCs. The results in Fig. 3f show that with thickness up to 0.3 cm, the optical efficiencies improve dramatically as LSC thickness increases. When the LSC thickness is ≥ 0.4 cm, the optical efficiency reaches a plateau due to the balance between absorption, re-absorption and emission events, similar to the trend observed in the laminated LSCs in Fig. 3e. At fixed absorption values, the overall thickness (interlayer + substrates) of LSC plays a significant role in reducing the re-absorption effect for an improved optical efficiency. The simulated optical efficiency of full polymer LSC with the overall thickness of 0.5 cm is 4.53 ± 0.18%, close to the one of L-LSC with 1 mm-thick interlayer (overall thickness = 0.5 cm) of 4.50 ± 0.16%. The similar values of optical efficiencies for the two different structures indicate that if the absorption value is kept constant in the different devices, the type of device architecture does not affect the optical efficiency at the same overall LSC/L-LSC thickness. Further evidence is reported in Fig. S6, where PMMA substrates with various thicknesses are used. Overall, at a fixed absorption of the emissive layer, the thinner the overall thickness of LSC/L-LSC, the greater the re-absorption losses as the density of the QDs is higher, and vice versa. This can be further supported by the experimental evidence that L-LSC-2 mm with the highest overall thickness (0.6 cm) exhibited the best
η
opt
= 4.32% among other prepared L-LSCs (for L-LSC-0.5 mm and L-LSC-1 mm,
η
opt
=
3.46% and 4.02%, respectively). Therefore, thickness optimization in the LSC construction could help to minimize the reabsorption loss. Moreover, in the conventional full polymer LSC, long-time complex polymerization process is required, which inevitably introduces uncontrollable defects. This is further accentuated for thick-polymer LSC. Replacing the polymer with clear optical substrates to maintain a minimum thickness of interlayer could reduce the formation of impurities and aggregations from phase transformation. The experimental values of optical efficiencies are lower than those of the simulations (Fig. 3e and f), which is probably attributed to the energy loss from air gaps between devices and solar cell without the refractive index matching glue.

To further add the possibility to tune the appearance of L-LSCs, and thus use them as energy-smart windows in buildings, we designed color-tunable multi-layers L-LSCs in a single device. In this work, we used colloidal CsPbX3 (X = Cl, Br, I) perovskite QDs due to their high PLQY and relatively high color purity in blue, green and red as additive primary colors. Their optical spectra are shown in Fig. 4b. The blue (CsPbClBr2), green (CsPbBr3) and red (CsPb(Br0.2I0.8)3) perovskites QDs have emission peaks at 480 nm, 505 nm and 658 nm, respectively. In the fabrication process, as shown in Fig. 4a, each interlayer (0.5 mm-thick) embedding different QDs is compressed between two substrates (PMMA) to form a multilayered sandwich structure. Considering that the laminated devices with different interlayer thicknesses exhibited similar performance, the device configuration with 0.5 mm-thick interlayer was chosen on the basis of the shortest polymerization time and highest transparency compared to other configurations, that could represent an advantage for their future commercialization. A single-interlayered L-LSC is formed by an emissive interlayer between two PMMA substrates (3 layers in total), while double-interlayered L-LSCs are built by three PMMA substrates compressing two distinct emissive interlayers (5 layers in total). By tuning the ratios of three different primary colors through adjusting the QDs concentrations/types in each interlayer (Fig. S7), color-tunable L-LSCs can be realized. In this work, to better compare the device performance between the different structures, the amount of each QD type used in a single L-LSC was kept constant (3.5 mg), for a total amount of QDs in each multilayered L-LSC of 7 mg.

Single primary color-based L-LSCs were also fabricated for comparison (Fig. 4c). Also in this case, 3.5 mg QDs were used in each device. Each of the “pure” blue (B), green (G) and red (R) L-LSCs exhibited a very bright emission from the edges under both UV and solar light illumination. By stacking different emissive interlayers, several double-interlayered L-LSCs with diverse color appearances can be prepared (Fig. 4d-f). For example, by stacking blue and red QDs, the double-interlayered L-LSC shows an orange appearance under solar light but magenta color under UV radiation (Fig. 4d), while blue and green interlayers can form a L-LSC with lime color under solar light but cyan appearance under UV radiation (Fig. 4e). Other colorful L-LSCs can be obtained by controlling the wavelengths combination, which can be easily realized by tuning the QDs concentrations/ratios (Fig. S7) and the emission wavelengths of the embedded QDs. Another possibility to obtain color-tunable devices would be by physically mixing different types of QDs in a single emissive layer. In Fig. 4g, we fabricated single-interlayered L-LSC containing two different types of QDs by physically mixing them. As expected, mixed blue and green QDs based L-LSC shows a cyan appearance under UV irradiation, like the multilayered L-LSC. However, in the case of mixed QDs based L-LSC, a severe emission quenching of QDs can be observed. Such QD emission quenching will negatively affect the appearance of the LSC and reduce the final PCE of the device.

The PCE and optical efficiency of the representative multilayered L-LSCs have been measured. To efficiently absorb solar light, the QDs emissive layers with smaller absorption range were used as top interlayer in the double-interlayered devices. Fig. 5a reports the J-V curves of the double-interlayered L-LSCs with separated QDs, as well as single-interlayered L-LSCs with physically mixed QDs. The results show that all the double-interlayered L-LSCs exhibit higher efficiencies compared to single-interlayered L-LSCs. Specifically, the PCE (0.31%) of Separated-BR L-LSC is 2 times higher than that of Mixed-BR L-LSC (0.10%), while the
η
opt
(4.30%) is twice as high (2.05%). Among all the color-tunable devices, the double-interlayered L-LSCs exhibited better performance compared to single-interlayered ones, probably resulting from the reduced re-absorption loss due to the increased overall thickness and the stacked interlayers that avoid QDs emission quenching. Furthermore, among the double-interlayered L-LSCs, Separated-GR L-LSC achieved the highest PCE (0.34%) and
η
opt
(4.97%) compared to Separated-BR L-LSC (PCE=0.31%,
η
opt
= 4.30%) and Separated-BG L-LSC (PCE=0.30%,
η
opt
= 4.46%). This can probably be attributed to the broad absorption range of green and red QDs, and the relatively small overlap of their absorption and emission spectra. The average EQE curves and corresponding
J
sc
Int
are reported in Fig. S9 a-b and Table S2. All the double-interlayered L-LSCs with separated QDs emissive layers present higher
J
sc
Int
than single-interlayered L-LSC with mixed QDs. Particularly, the
J
sc
Int
value of Separated-BR L-LSC (1.42 mA cm−2) is more than two times as high as that of Mixed-BR L-LSC (0.65 mA cm−2), similar to the experimental values. The EQE values at 350–500 nm of Mixed-BR L-LSC dropped to almost zero, indicating that no absorption occurs in this range or/and the absorbed light in this range does not transfer to the PV cell due to the failed downshift process. This result is consistent with Fig. 4g, where the Mixed-BR L-LSC exhibited the weakest photoluminescence under UV light, likely due to the emission quenching of physically mixed QDs.

We propose a possible light propagation mechanism to explain the enhanced performance of multilayered L-LSC structures built by stacking double QDs interlayers between substrates. As shown in Fig. 5b, when incident solar light strikes the surface of the Mixed-GR L-LSC, only a portion of the impinging light is absorbed by the single interlayer containg mixed green and red QDs, while most of the light is lost as it passes through the waveguide. In single-interlayered L-LSC, physically concentrating different types of QDs into a thin interlayer could trigger uncontrollable and unpredictable QDs quenching, thereby leading to a decrease in the absorption and emission of the interlayer. In this work, QDs quenching is observed in single-interlayered devices, which is proved by the absorption spectra of Mixed-GR L-LSC shown in Fig. S8, where the absorption dropped to near zero after 550 mm. By contrast, with a double-interlayered architecture, the first interlayer containing green QDs absorb part of the incident light first. The unabsorbed light then passes through the top layers and directly hits the second interlayer, where the red QDs can absorb more light. Furthermore, double-interlayered L-LSCs have a higher overall thickness (0.7 cm) compared to single-interlayered L-LSCs (0.45 cm). When both devices contain the same types and quantity of QDs, the re-absorption loss is reduced in the thicker device. Therefore, double-interlayered L-LSCs represent an improved solution compared to single-interlayer devices as they enhance the device’s absorption and promote photon propagation.

As aforementioned, high aesthetic quality for a L-LSC is also a vital factor to increase their acceptance in the urban environment, thus we evaluated the aesthetic qualities of the designed multilayered L-LSCs (Fig. 5c-d and Table S2). Excluding the BR L-LSC sample, other kinds of double-interlayered L-LSCs presented higher AVT compared to single-interlayered L-LSCs with mixed QDs. The better AVT values can be ascribed to the suppressed QDs aggregations as the QDs are physically separated in two different layers.

To evaluate the color quality of L-LSCs, their color rendering index (CRI) was calculated based on the transmittance properties of the L-LSC devices. In general, for an acceptable color quality, a CRI value of 80 is required. The single-interlayered L-LSCs exhibited a CRI value of 72.61 and 82.8 for Mixed-BG L-LSC and Mixed-GR L-LSC, respectively. Instead, by using the multi-interlayered structure, higher CRI values of 75.11 and 86.77 can be obtained for Separated-BG L-LSC and Separated-GR L-LSC, respectively. Among all the fabricated double-interlayered L-LSCs, Separated-GR L-LSC exhibited the highest CRI of 86.77 (which also achieved a highest PCE of 0.34% in the optical performance test). To better show the color appearances of the devices, all the fabricated L-LSC with multilayers were fully exposed to simulated sunlight, using a white background as color reference (Fig. S10).[74] Tuning the QDs types in double-interlayered L-LSCs results in the device color changes, which can be reflected as positive increases of a* and b* in CIELAB color space. For example, as shown in Fig. 5d by replacing red QDs with green QDs as the second interlayer could change the device color from orange into lime, and switch the (a*, b*) coordinate from (0.81, 38.70) to (1.41, 32.31).

In summary, an easy, cost-effective fabrication method to prepare multilayered L-LSCs is presented. We optimized the fabrication parameters of L-LSCs by changing the substrate materials and tuning the thicknesses of emissive interlayers. The results showed that with the same interlayer thickness of 0.5 mm, PMMA based L-LSC exhibited higher PCE (0.24%) and optical efficiency (
η
opt
=3.46%) compared to green glass-based L-LSC (PCE=0.09%,
η
opt
=1.76%) due to better optical properties and good coupling with the emissive interlayers. Moreover, it was observed that the efficiency of a device is mainly proportional to the L-LSC’s overall thickness, assuming constant device surface area and emissive material amount (i.e. the final absorption value is kept constant). To optimize the fabrication and performance of L-LSCs, the thicknesses of interlayers and overall devices were varied. At a fixed QDs concentration, using clear optical waveguides combined with an optimized polymer interlayer thickness of 0.5 cm reduces the energy loss. This also allows to reduce polymer impurities/defects and optimize the fabrication time. Based on these findings, we designed a color-tunable multilayered L-LSC structure. By stacking different types of QDs based emissive layers and tuning the QDs concentrations/ratios, the color appearance of the device can be controlled. The optimized device exhibited not only an enhanced PCE and
η
opt
due to reduced re-absorption loss, but also relatively high aesthetic quality.

Overall, the fabrication method to build color-tunable multilayered L-LSC structures reported in this work is universal and can be applied to several QDs which have good compatibility with PMMA and PLMA. Based on the optimizations reported, our work could offer precious information for developing buildings integrated laminated LSC windows with custom LSC appearance configurations.
